thread.title: Trump's Bedminster liquor license saved by election
thread.title_full: Trump's Bedminster liquor license saved by election

author: W. JACOB PERRY Staff Writer
title: Trump's Bedminster liquor license saved by election
text:
BEDMINSTER TWP. – Donald Trump’s election as president not only appears to have terminated federal criminal cases against him, [but saved the liquor license](https://www.newjerseyhills.com/bernardsville_news/news/hearing-to-terminate-trumps-bedminster-liquor-license-delayed/article_03cfc842-486b-11ef-b73f-ffade4a5c110.html) at Trump National Golf Club on Lamington Road.
The license was put in jeopardy in May. That month, a New York jury found Trump guilty on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records related to a 2016 hush money payment to a porn star to protect his first campaign for president.
On June 28, the New Jersey Attorney General’s Division of Alcoholic Beverage Control (ABC), citing the felony convictions, said it would not renew liquor licenses for Trump-owned golf courses in Bedminster and Colts Neck in Monmouth County.
The licenses were to expire June 30, but ABC issued a temporary 90-day permit to let the clubs continue to serve alcohol pending a hearing. That hearing was scheduled for July 19 in Trenton.
The hearing, however, was contingent on Trump being sentenced for the New York State convictions. Although Trump’s political opponents have referred to him as a convicted felon, an individual is not legally a convicted felon until the court imposes a sentence.
New York state Superior Court Judge Juan Merchan initially scheduled sentencing for July 11 but then postponed it to assess the impact of a U.S. Supreme Court ruling on presidential immunity.
Trump’s Nov. 5 election as president created an additional hurdle because there are constitutional questions as to whether a state judge can sentence a president-elect or a sitting president.
On Tuesday, Nov. 12, the Manhattan District Attorney’s Office sent a letter to Judge Merchan in which it said it would agree to postpone sentencing until after Trump’s four-year term as president concludes in January 2029.
The next day, Trump’s defense filed a request with the judge that would allow it to file a motion for dismissal, which the District Attorney’s Office opposes. Judge Merchan granted the request and required the motion to be filed by Monday, Dec. 2, with prosecutors needing to respond by Monday, Dec. 9.
As for the status of Trump’s New Jersey liquor licenses, the state Attorney General’s press office, in an email response to an inquiry from this newspaper on Tuesday, Nov. 19, said the interim permits for the two Trump liquor licenses “will be extended until the matter is heard and decided.
“There are no updates at this time,” the email said. “The hearing date for the licensing renewal is dependent on the sentencing date in the New York State Court.”
‘Moral Turpitude’
Back on June 10, when ABC first announced the Trump liquor license evaluations, it cited a New Jersey law that states, “No license of any class shall be issued to any person under the age of 18 years or to any person who has been convicted of a crime involving moral turpitude.”
The next day, the Trump Organization issued a statement saying Trump does not hold any liquor licenses in New Jersey, nor is he an officer or director in any entity that holds a liquor license in the state.
The Bedminster and Colts Neck golf course liquor licenses were issued in the name of corporate entities Lamington Farm Club LLC, and Trump National Golf Course Colts Neck LLC, respectively.
The state Attorney General’s Office later rejected the argument that the liquor licenses were not tied to Trump.
The Attorney General’s office said “a review by ABC indicates that Mr. Trump maintains a direct beneficial interest” in the liquor licenses “through the receipt of revenues and profits from them, as the sole beneficiary of the Donald J. Trump Revocable Trust.”
The Attorney General’s office said it has “discretion to deny an application based on a review of the applicant’s background and character,” and that the felony convictions “raise concerns as to whether this licensee remains qualified to hold said license.”
The ABC effort had drawn some political pushback.
On Aug. 15, Republican gubernatorial candidate Jack Ciattarelli, a former state Assemblyman and Somerset County freeholder, and Monmouth County Commissioner Thomas Arnone, also a Republican, denounced the review in a joint letter to state Attorney General Matt Platkin and ABC Director Kirstin Krueger.
They said “the long history of animus towards former President Trump” by Gov. Phil Murphy and his administration indicates that “the thrust of your eﬀort is political in nature.
“As residents and taxpayers – as well as former and current elected oﬃcials representing Monmouth and Somerset Counties, respectively – we urge you to not allow partisan politics to impact the livelihoods of the several hundred working class New Jersey residents employed at both golf clubs.”
